message.content: You are a hostile Rust code reviewer. Review ALL source at /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/ (main.rs, platform.rs, backend.rs, config.rs, github.rs, docker_backend.rs, service.rs) plus Cargo.toml for REAL bugs that would bite in production: incorrect error handling, race conditions (e.g. serve loop vs stop, ensure_count counting exited-but-not-removed containers), wrong docker/gh CLI usage (flag syntax, output parsing fragility — docker ps --format json availability, df parsing), edge cases (count>1, org scope, config with cpus exceeding daemon after VM resize, memory-swap semantics), the unique_suffix collision risk, stop_all deregistering OTHER hosts' ezgha-* runners (org/multi-host hazard), managed_containers missing exited containers still holding registrations. Only report defects with a concrete failure scenario. Severity by impact.

content.text: All files read. Now verifying a few version-dependent claims before reporting.

input.command: docker --version 2>/dev/null; docker info --format '{{.DockerRootDir}}' 2>/dev/null; ls -d /var/lib/docker 2>&1 | head -1; docker ps --format json 2>&1 | head -2
input.description: Check local docker version and format json behavior

content.content: [REDACTED for publication: verbatim content from a private repository (worldarchitect.ai self-hosted runner infrastructure) was returned by this tool call and has been removed. The reviewer's analysis of it is preserved in subsequent assistant messages.]

findings.title: free_disk_gb() checks the HOST filesystem at the DAEMON's path — disk-floor guard silently fails open in exactly the VM setups the tool targets
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:159-181. `free_disk_gb()` asks the daemon for `{{.DockerRootDir}}` (a path INSIDE the daemon's mount namespace, e.g. `/var/lib/docker` inside the Colima/Lima/Docker Desktop VM) and then runs host-side `df -Pk` against that string. Three concrete failures: (1) macOS Docker Desktop / Colima: `/var/lib/docker` does not exist on the host, `df` exits non-zero, function returns `None`, and `ensure_count` (docker_backend.rs:192) SKIPS the disk check entirely — the guard the code comments call 'the dominant self-hosted runner failure mode' is a silent no-op. (2) Linux host that also has a native `/var/lib/docker` directory but uses a Colima context: `df` measures the host root fs (plenty free) while the VM disk is 100% full — guard passes, spawns more runners onto a full VM disk, jobs fail with ENOSPC. This is precisely the 2026-06-14 colima disk-fill incident class. (3) daemon_capacity() at docker_backend.rs:36 already documents that the daemon may be a VM, so the codebase knows about this and still gets it wrong for disk.
findings.recommendation: Query disk through the daemon, not the host: `docker system df --format` or run `df` inside a throwaway container (`docker run --rm alpine df -Pk /`), or read `docker info` disk fields where available. At minimum, treat `None` from free_disk_gb() as a hard error (fail closed) instead of skipping the check, since the guard is the product's core promise.

findings.title: stop_all() deregisters OTHER hosts' ezgha runners — org/multi-host `ezgha stop` is a fleet-wide outage button
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:148-154. After removing local containers, `stop_all` lists ALL runners on the target and removes every one whose name starts with `ezgha-` and `!r.busy`. Runner names are `ezgha-{hostname}-{suffix}` (docker_backend.rs:67), so with org scope (or a shared repo) host A's `ezgha stop` deregisters host B's and host C's idle runners too. Concrete scenario: 3 hosts each serving `count=2` against org `jleechanorg`; operator runs `ezgha stop` on host A to take it down for maintenance → all 6 idle registrations are deleted; hosts B/C's waiting listeners are severed server-side, their containers exit, and queued jobs sit with zero available runners until B/C's 30s serve loops re-register. Repeated stops cause continuous org-wide runner churn. Additionally the busy check is a stale snapshot: a runner can pick up a job between list_runners() and remove_runner() (GitHub returns 422 for busy runners, which mitigates but produces spurious errors that are silently swallowed by `let _ =`).
findings.recommendation: Filter by this host's prefix: `r.name.starts_with(&format!("ezgha-{}-", hostname()))`. Better: record the runner_id label (`ezgha.runner_id` is already set on containers at docker_backend.rs:76) and deregister exactly the ids belonging to containers this host just removed.

findings.title: `docker ps --format json` is a literal Go template on Docker CLI < 23.0 — every container operation permanently fails
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:116-128. On Docker CLI 20.10 (Ubuntu 22.04 / Debian 11 `docker.io` package, still widely deployed on the exact commodity hosts this tool targets) `--format json` is interpreted as a literal template and prints the string `json` once per container. `serde_json::from_str("json")` then fails with 'unexpected docker ps json', so `managed_containers()` errors → `ensure_count`, `stop_all`, and `Status` are ALL broken. In `serve` this manifests as an infinite 'ensure_count failed (will retry)' loop every 30s that never starts a single runner; `ezgha stop` errors out and removes nothing. The shorthand `--format json` was only added in CLI v23.0 (before that you must write `--format '{{json .}}'`). Verified NDJSON output on local 29.4.3; the pre-23 literal behavior is the documented CLI change. Related fragility in github.rs:41: `-f "labels[]=x"` array syntax requires gh >= 2.27; on older gh it sends a scalar field literally named `labels[]` and generate-jitconfig 422s on every spawn attempt.
findings.recommendation: Use `--format '{{json .}}'` which works on all CLI versions that support formatting, or fail fast with a version check in `doctor`/`platform::detect()` (minimum docker CLI 23.0, minimum gh 2.27) so the operator gets one clear error instead of a silent retry loop.

findings.title: Crash-looping runner container leaks unbounded JIT registrations — no backoff, no visibility, no cleanup
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:66-102, 187-207. `start_one` only cleans up the server-side JIT registration when `docker run` itself exits non-zero (line 91-98). If `docker run -d` succeeds but the container dies immediately afterwards — wrong-arch image on Apple Silicon, image missing `./run.sh`, expired/garbled jitconfig, OOM during runner bootstrap — the `--rm` flag erases the container, `managed_containers()` (docker ps, no `-a`) can never observe that it existed, and the offline JIT registration is orphaned on GitHub. The serve loop then sees `alive=0` and spawns a fresh registration+container every 30 seconds with zero backoff: ~2,880 orphaned offline `ezgha-*` registrations per day per configured runner, plus continuous generate-jitconfig API calls burning rate limit. `Status` shows the zombie list growing but nothing reaps them; only a manual `ezgha stop` (which also nukes other hosts, see separate finding) cleans up. The failure is silent from serve's perspective — it prints 'respawned ephemeral runner X' every cycle as if things were healthy.
findings.recommendation: (1) Drop `--rm` and use `docker ps -a` + explicit reaping so exited containers are observable and their `ezgha.runner_id` labels can drive targeted deregistration. (2) Add exponential backoff / a circuit breaker when a runner dies within N seconds of spawn. (3) Periodically reconcile: list `ezgha-{hostname}-*` runners that are offline with no corresponding container and remove them.

findings.title: `ezgha stop` races the serve loop and does not stop the installed service — runners resurrect within 30 seconds
findings.detail: main.rs:192-217, service.rs. `InstallService` installs a systemd --user unit with `Restart=on-failure` / launchd `KeepAlive=true` running `ezgha serve`, whose loop calls `ensure_count` every 30s forever (main.rs:201-211). `Commands::Stop` only calls `stop_all()` — it never touches the service or signals the serve process. Concrete scenario: operator installed the service, later runs `ezgha stop` during an incident (e.g. the disk is full and they want runners GONE); the command prints 'removed N managed container(s)' and exits 0, and ≤30s later the serve daemon re-registers and re-spawns everything. During an actual disk-exhaustion incident the disk-floor bail in ensure_count may prevent respawn, but for every other stop reason (security incident, runaway job, maintenance) stop is a lie. There is also no locking between a foreground `ezgha serve`/`ezgha start` and the service instance: both read `managed_containers()` then spawn the shortfall, so two concurrent processes can each see alive=0 and both spawn `count` runners (2x capacity).
findings.recommendation: Make `stop` stop/disable the service (systemctl --user stop ezgha / launchctl unload) or at least detect a running service and refuse with instructions. Add a file lock (e.g. flock on a state file under XDG runtime dir) around ensure_count so serve/start/stop serialize.

findings.title: MANAGED_LABEL is global, not per-target — multi-config hosts miscount capacity and stop kills the wrong runners
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:10, 74-75, 116-138, 141-156. The CLI explicitly supports `--config <path>` (main.rs:22), inviting one host to serve multiple targets (repo A and org B). But every container carries only `ezgha=managed` with no target discriminator, and `managed_containers()` filters solely on that label. Concrete failures: (1) capacity starvation — serve for repo A runs 2 containers; serve for org B (count=2) counts those 2 as its own, sees alive>=count, and never spawns a single runner for B, silently; (2) cross-target kill — `ezgha stop --config B.toml` runs `docker rm -f` on repo A's runner containers, aborting A's in-flight jobs mid-run (docker rm -f during a busy job = job failure on GitHub).
findings.recommendation: Add a target-scoped label (e.g. `ezgha.target=<scope>/<target>` or a hash of the config path) at `docker run` time and filter managed_containers()/stop_all() on it.

findings.title: list_runners() fetches only the first 100 runners — no pagination
findings.detail: github.rs:73-87. `?per_page=100` with a single `gh api` call. An org with >100 registered runners (large fleets, or the registration-leak finding above filling the list with orphans — the two bugs compound) means `Status` undercounts and `stop_all` fails to deregister ezgha runners that happen to sort beyond page 1. Because GitHub returns runners in a stable order, the same runners are persistently invisible, not randomly.
findings.recommendation: Use `gh api --paginate` (and `--slurp` or concatenated-array handling for the merged output), or loop pages until `runners.len() < per_page`.

findings.title: effective_limits clamps memory/cpus to 100% of daemon capacity — 'clamped' container can still OOM the docker VM
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:50-63. When config exceeds daemon capacity (the stated 'VM resized down after init' case), the clamp sets the container limit EQUAL to total daemon memory/NCPU. A Colima VM with 4 GB total gets a container allowed to consume all 4 GB; the VM kernel, dockerd, containerd and any sibling container get nothing, so the VM OOM-killer fires on the daemon itself — reproducing the exact host-takedown failure mode the tool exists to prevent, one level down. `--memory-swap` set equal to `--memory` (docker_backend.rs:79-80) is correct hard-limit usage per se, but note that on cgroup-v1 hosts without `swapaccount=1` docker silently ignores it and the job can swap-thrash.
findings.recommendation: Clamp to a fraction with headroom, mirroring what Init already does: `ncpu - 1` (min 1) and `daemon_mem - reserve` (e.g. 75-80% or minus 1 GB). Warn loudly when a clamp fires under serve, not just eprintln once per 30s loop iteration.

findings.title: systemd --user service dies on logout / never starts on headless hosts (no linger), and After=docker.service is a no-op in the user manager
findings.detail: service.rs:24-58. (1) User units only run while the user has an active session unless `loginctl enable-linger <user>` is set; on a headless runner box (the normal deployment) the service never starts after reboot until someone SSHes in, and stops when they log out — runners silently vanish. (2) `After=network-online.target docker.service` references SYSTEM units from a USER unit; the user manager has no `docker.service`, so the ordering is silently ignored. Mitigated at boot by Restart=on-failure/RestartSec=10 (serve fails while docker is down and retries), but combined with (1) the 'install and forget' promise doesn't hold.
findings.recommendation: Run `loginctl enable-linger` during install (or document it and verify in `doctor`). Drop the ineffective After= deps or note that restart-retry is the actual mechanism.

findings.title: Runner name built from unvalidated hostname — FQDN or long hostnames break generate-jitconfig on every spawn, forever
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:24-31, 67. `ezgha-{hostname}-{suffix}` with hostname from the `hostname` command, no truncation or sanitization. GitHub runner names are capped at 64 chars; a host whose hostname resolves to an FQDN (common on cloud images, e.g. `ip-10-0-1-23.us-west-2.compute.internal`, 39 chars) plus `ezgha-` (6) + `-` + up to ~16 hex chars sits right at/over the limit, and longer corporate FQDNs blow past it. Result: generate-jitconfig 422s on every ensure_count cycle; serve prints the same error every 30s and never runs a job. The name is also used as the docker container name, which forbids chars like `/` that `hostname` can technically emit in weird configs.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize to `[a-zA-Z0-9-]`, take the short hostname (split at first `.`), and truncate so the full runner name stays ≤64 chars.

findings.title: unique_suffix() has no randomness or PID and uses ambiguous variable-width hex concatenation
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:12-22. The suffix is `{secs:x}{nanos:x}` from TWO separate `SystemTime::now()` calls (so it doesn't even describe one instant), with no PID, counter, or random component, and unpadded concatenation is ambiguous (e.g. secs=0xab,nanos=0xc and secs=0xa,nanos=0xbc both yield 'abc'). Concrete collision paths: (a) two hosts with the same hostname (default cloud images all named `ubuntu`) spawning in the same nanosecond window — runner NAME collision at GitHub causes generate-jitconfig to fail or shadow; (b) two local processes (serve + manual `ezgha start`, see locking finding) racing — docker `--name` conflict makes `docker run` fail, which is at least cleaned up via remove_runner. Probability is low per-event but the fix is one line, and the failure mode when it hits is a confusing intermittent spawn error.
findings.recommendation: Append `std::process::id()` and a per-process atomic counter, or 8 random hex chars; use zero-padded `{secs:08x}-{nanos:08x}` if timestamps stay.

findings.title: stop_all() reports success for containers it failed to remove, and deregisters runners whose containers survived
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:141-156. `let _ = Command::new("docker").args(["rm", "-f", &c.id])` swallows failures (daemon hiccup, permission change, container in un-killable D-state), then unconditionally returns `containers.len()` — `ezgha stop` prints 'removed 3 managed container(s)' even if 0 were removed. Worse, the code then deregisters all idle ezgha runners anyway: a surviving container's runner listener gets severed server-side while the container keeps running as an orphan consuming its memory/cpu reservation until manually cleaned.
findings.recommendation: Check `docker rm -f` exit status per container, count only successes, report failures, and only deregister runner_ids whose containers were confirmed removed (the `ezgha.runner_id` label is already available).

findings.title: JIT config (runner credential) exposed via container argv — readable by docker inspect and process listings
findings.detail: docker_backend.rs:88. The encoded JIT config is passed as a `docker run` command argument, so it persists in `docker inspect <container>` output and appears in host `ps` output during spawn. Anyone with docker-socket access or host ps visibility can lift a live runner credential and register a rogue listener to intercept the job intended for that runner (with the repo/org's secrets exposed to it). Docker-group users are root-equivalent anyway, which caps severity, but rootless-docker and audit-logging environments leak it further (argv commonly lands in shell/audit logs).
findings.recommendation: Pass it via `--env-file` with a mode-0600 tempfile, or stdin, per the actions-runner image's supported `ACTIONS_RUNNER_INPUT_JITCONFIG`-style env path — anything that keeps it out of argv.

findings.title: `ezgha init` silently overwrites an existing tuned config, and org-scope target is unvalidated
findings.detail: main.rs:102-138, config.rs:120-126. Re-running `init` (e.g. following a README on an already-configured host) clobbers hand-tuned limits, labels, image, and min_free_disk_gb with defaults — no prompt, no backup. Separately, `--scope org --target owner/repo` passes validation (only repo scope checks for '/'), producing `orgs/owner/repo` API paths that 404 on every gh call; the first error surfaces later as a confusing generate-jitconfig failure rather than at init. Also, when `count > 1` and start_one fails mid-loop, ensure_count returns Err and drops the names of runners it DID start (docker_backend.rs:201-206), so `ezgha start` reports pure failure while runners are actually running.
findings.recommendation: Refuse to overwrite an existing config without `--force`; reject '/' in org-scope targets at init; on partial ensure_count failure, report the runners already started alongside the error.

input.summary: Hostile review of ez-gh-actions (7 source files + Cargo.toml, ~1160 LOC). Two critical findings: (1) the disk-floor guard — the tool's core safety promise — runs `df` on the HOST against the DAEMON's DockerRootDir path, so it silently fails open (returns None → check skipped) on Colima/Docker Desktop and can read the wrong (host) filesystem while the docker VM disk is full, reproducing the exact disk-fill incident class the tool exists to prevent; (2) `ezgha stop` deregisters ALL idle `ezgha-*` runners on the target, including other hosts' runners in org scope — a fleet-wide outage button. Four majors: `docker ps --format json` is a literal template on Docker CLI <23.0 (Ubuntu 22.04 docker.io) breaking every container operation with a permanent 30s retry loop; crash-looping `--rm` containers leak unbounded orphaned JIT registrations (~2880/day) with no backoff because exited containers are unobservable and start_one only cleans up on docker-run failure; `stop` neither stops the installed serve service (runners resurrect ≤30s) nor holds any lock against concurrent serve/start double-spawning; and the `ezgha=managed` label is not target-scoped, so multi-config hosts miscount capacity (one target silently starves) and stop kills the other target's in-flight jobs. Eight minors with concrete scenarios: no runner-list pagination (>100 runners), clamping limits to 100% of daemon capacity (VM OOM), systemd --user needs enable-linger on headless hosts (+ ineffective After=docker.service), unvalidated hostname breaking the 64-char runner-name limit (perpetual 422 loop on FQDN hosts), collision-prone unique_suffix (no PID/randomness, ambiguous concat), stop_all reporting false success and deregistering runners whose containers survived, JIT credentials exposed in argv/docker inspect, and init silently clobbering tuned configs plus unvalidated org targets. Cargo.toml itself is clean.
